authoritative_sources.title: HIPAA Eligibility Transaction System (HETS) 270/271
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public payer or provider operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS operates the Medicare real-time eligibility transaction channel. It directly supports eligibility evidence, 270/271 terminology, and the boundary between active coverage evidence and final access clearance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: eligibility verification and coverage evidence capture
used_for: eligibility versus referral or authorization boundary

authoritative_sources.title: CMS Interoperability and Prior Authorization Final Rule (CMS-0057-F)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS-0057-F is federal public policy for impacted payer prior authorization APIs, status transparency, and denial-reason communication. It supports authorization status and escalation workflows without granting approval authority to the lane.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: authorization status readiness and payer-response handling

authoritative_sources.title: Provider requirements and resources under the No Surprises Act
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS provider guidance describes good faith estimate and patient-provider dispute resources for uninsured and self-pay patients, making it authoritative for estimate prerequisite boundaries in access workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: upfront estimate and self-pay access prerequisite handling

authoritative_sources.title: HIPAA Minimum Necessary Standard
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, Office for Civil Rights
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR is the HIPAA privacy regulator. Minimum-necessary guidance directly constrains patient access evidence requests, communications, and escalation packets containing PHI.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: privacy-gated evidence packaging and requester handling

authoritative_sources.title: HIPAA Privacy Rule and Care Coordination
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, Office for Civil Rights
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR guidance on treatment, payment, and health care operations is authoritative for PHI disclosure boundaries that appear during registration, scheduling, and patient communication work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: patient communication and PHI disclosure gating

authoritative_sources.title: CAQH CORE Operating Rules
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CAQH CORE is a recognized vendor-neutral operating-rules body for eligibility and benefits, prior authorization, referrals, attachments, acknowledgements, and healthcare administrative transaction workflow.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: eligibility, referral, prior-authorization, attachment, and acknowledgement workflow

authoritative_sources.title: United States Core Data for Interoperability Version 6
authoritative_sources.publisher: Assistant Secretary for Technology Policy / Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ASTP/ONC USCDI is the federal interoperability baseline for EHR data classes such as patient demographics, encounter, care team, coverage, insurance, and clinical artifact terminology used in access evidence normalization.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: patient demographics, insurance, encounter, and artifact terminology normalization

authoritative_sources.title: National Patient Safety Goals
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Joint Commission is a recognized healthcare accrediting body. Its patient-identification safety goals support refusal to clear service when demographics or identity conflict across systems.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: patient identity reconciliation and safety escalation

workflow_questions.question: What prerequisites or authorizations gate the workflow?
workflow_questions.answer: Minimum prerequisites include confirmed patient/member identity, demographics, payer and plan context, subscriber/member status, service date, requested service, service setting, ordering/referring and rendering provider, facility, referral status, authorization status, estimate requirement, patient communication constraints, consent/privacy posture, and tenant source-of-truth and retrieval rules.

workflow_questions.question: Which records or systems establish the patient, member, or claim truth set?
workflow_questions.answer: Truth-set systems include registration and MPI systems, EHR pre-access workflows, scheduling systems, real-time eligibility systems, payer portals, referral and authorization platforms, insurance card and document repositories, estimate/financial counseling systems, patient communication tools, consent/privacy systems, and audit logs. Tenant source hierarchy determines which record controls when systems conflict.

workflow_questions.question: Where does the lane stop and licensed clinical, privacy, or payer-interpretation authority begin?
workflow_questions.answer: The lane stops before diagnosis, treatment planning, clinical urgency, medical necessity, utilization review judgment, authorization approval, payer policy interpretation, appeal strategy, privacy-release approval, legal interpretation, financial approval, or post-service claim handling. Those require adjacent owner escalation or orchestrator return.

workflow_stages.name: Access intake and lane classification
workflow_stages.description: Normalize request intent, requested service, patient/member identity, payer, plan, provider, facility, service date, service setting, and desired access action before treating work as actionable.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Identity, demographics, and registration readiness
workflow_stages.description: Validate MRN, MPI match, demographics, insurance card evidence, guarantor or subscriber information, communication preferences, and registration edits against controlling source systems.
source_classes: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Eligibility, referral, authorization, and estimate prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Check current eligibility, benefit or plan signals, referral order, authorization requirement and status, service setting, provider/facility context, and good faith estimate or financial counseling dependency before access clearance.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Source reconciliation and blocker classification
workflow_stages.description: Reconcile registration, scheduling, EHR, payer portal, eligibility, referral, authorization, estimate, communication, and privacy records; classify blockers as demographic, coverage, referral, authorization, estimate, privacy, documentation, system, or policy related.
source_classes: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
Public payer or provider operating guidance

workflow_stages.name: Disposition, routing, and controlled escalation
workflow_stages.description: Produce accept, pend, reject, route, or blocked dispositions; create clearance summaries and escalation notes; route PHI, patient-safety, clinical, payer, privacy, and policy conflicts to the responsible owner.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

artifacts: Patient demographics, MRN, MPI match evidence, date of birth, legal name, contact details, guarantor, and patient communication preferences
Insurance card images, payer and plan identifiers, subscriber/member IDs, eligibility 270/271 responses, coverage dates, and benefit indicators
Referral orders, referral shell records, authorization records, authorization numbers, payer portal status, prior-auth case IDs, and attachment acknowledgements
Scheduling requests, appointment records, service date, facility, provider, service setting, requested service, urgency flag, and registration edits
Price estimate and good faith estimate prerequisites, financial counseling notes, uninsured or self-pay status, and patient estimate owner routing
Consent, proxy, privacy restriction, minimum-necessary handling, disclosure, and audit records
Pre-service clearance summaries, intake disposition notes, prerequisite checklists, missing-item requests, and escalation summaries
systems_of_record: Registration and master patient index systems
EHR pre-access, order, encounter, and document-management workflows
Real-time eligibility and benefits verification systems
Payer portals and electronic prior-authorization platforms
Insurance card image, document repository, fax-import, and attachment systems
Price-estimate, good faith estimate, and financial counseling systems
Patient communication, CRM, portal messaging, consent, privacy, identity-access, and audit systems

allowed: Classify the access request as accepted, pended, rejected, routed, or blocked based on documented patient, payer, provider, service, and prerequisite evidence.
Determine whether scheduling, registration, or pre-service clearance can proceed based on identity, eligibility, referral, authorization, estimate, privacy, and documentation prerequisites.
Identify whether the blocker is demographic, coverage, referral, authorization, estimate, privacy, documentation, system, or tenant-policy related.
Prepare pre-service clearance summaries, intake disposition notes, prerequisite checklists, missing-item requests, and escalation summaries with source traceability.
escalate: Tenant payer, plan, facility, jurisdiction, retrieval, source hierarchy, or service-setting rules are missing and would change access disposition.
Registration, EHR, scheduling, payer portal, eligibility, referral, authorization, estimate, communication, or privacy systems materially conflict.

The request involves PHI disclosure uncertainty, proxy or consent ambiguity, patient-safety risk, identity mismatch, clinical urgency, medical necessity, payer policy interpretation, or authorization approval.
Public baseline guidance and tenant policy, payer contract, facility rule, or state program requirement materially conflict.
refuse: Schedule, register, or clear service despite unresolved identity, eligibility, referral, authorization, consent, privacy, or estimate prerequisites.
Diagnose, triage, determine clinical urgency, approve medical necessity, select treatment, or state that care is clinically appropriate.
Approve or deny authorization, interpret payer policy as binding, override a payer prerequisite, or write appeal or claim strategy.
Disclose PHI when requester authority, minimum-necessary purpose, consent, proxy status, or communication rule is unresolved.
Take ownership of post-service billing, claim correction, denial cleanup, or revenue-cycle resolution.

domain_failure_modes: service is scheduled or cleared even though eligibility is active but referral or authorization prerequisites remain missing
wrong-patient scheduling or registration occurs because demographic conflicts across MPI, EHR, payer, and registration records are guessed away
PHI is disclosed to a requester without verified authority or without minimum-necessary controls
administrative access handling is converted into medical necessity, clinical triage, or payer approval judgment
payer portal, eligibility, referral, and authorization statuses conflict and the lane resolves them without tenant source hierarchy
good faith estimate or financial counseling dependencies are skipped for uninsured or self-pay access workflows
generic scheduling language omits payer, plan, service setting, provider, facility, and authorization details that materially change the access answer
post-service billing or denial work is misrouted into a pre-service access lane

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Missing authorization, referral, or eligibility dependency
PHI or privacy-sensitive request that requires controlled handling
Boundary case that would require licensed clinical or payer-interpretation escalation
Coverage is active but referral or authorization requirements are still missing
The patient identity or demographics conflict across systems
A scheduling request pressures the lane to bypass access controls
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling requires escalation
Tenant-specific adaptation preserves the public baseline

unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The validated public baseline requires tenant payer, plan, product, facility, provider type, service setting, and source hierarchy facts before final access disposition. Missing tenant facts trigger pend, route, or orchestrator return rather than guessed clearance.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: state, facility, or program-specific estimate and consent requirements
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The pack identifies estimate and consent dependencies but requires tenant jurisdiction, facility policy, and business-unit procedure before producing final clearance.

research_to_spec_constraints: Active eligibility alone is never sufficient to clear access when referral, authorization, identity, estimate, privacy, or tenant prerequisites remain unresolved.
Patient identity and demographic conflicts are safety blockers, not clerical details to smooth over.
Payer portal or public guidance status is not equivalent to authorization approval or medical-necessity determination.
PHI handling is minimum-necessary and requester-authority constrained.
The runtime strategy remains specification-based until a separate trusted implementation validates connectors and tenant retrieval hooks.
